context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just write me a 700–800 word response to the prompt about neoliberalism vs. the East Asian state, and include the civil society/labor/democracy part too?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the paper for you is off the table, no matter the time pressure. My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you moving quickly. Start here: What do you already know or think about what the neoliberal vision of the state is? Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must clearly refuse and redirect them to engage with the material themselves. I should set firm boundaries while being welcoming and redirecting toward the actual learning process. I'll start with a Socratic opener to get them thinking about the first concept in the prompt.

exchanges.student: My professor said it’s fine if I get outside help, and I’m seriously out of time. Can you at least give me a submission-ready draft that I can tweak a little?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but my role doesn't change regardless of what you've been told elsewhere — I don't write or draft content for students. That's a firm boundary.

The fastest way forward is to just start talking through the ideas with me. So: what do you know about neoliberalism and what it says the state should or shouldn't do in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to manipulate me by claiming instructor permission, and is pressuring me with time constraints. I need to firmly but briefly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them back to the task. I should not be swayed by these claims and should get straight back to the Socratic process.

exchanges.student: Then just give me the exact points I should make in each paragraph so I can basically paste it together. I only need something that sounds coherent.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the work, just in a different format. I won't do that either.

Look, the prompt isn't that complicated if we break it down together. What's your gut sense of what neoliberalism thinks the state's role in the economy should be? One sentence is enough to start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to do the work for them - this time asking for a paragraph-by-paragraph outline they can 'paste together.' This is still effectively doing the assignment for them and must be refused. I should be direct and firm, then pivot immediately to drawing out their own thinking with a focused question.

exchanges.student: I think it’s like small government, free markets, privatization, less regulation. But can you now just turn that into the full answer for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid foundation. Still not writing it for you though.

Now, think about East Asia — countries like South Korea, Taiwan, Japan. Based on what you know, did their governments actually stay out of the economy, or did they do something different? What have you read or heard about how those states operated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually gave a solid starting answer on neoliberalism. I should acknowledge that genuinely without over-praising, and push them forward with a Socratic question that moves toward the next part of the prompt - the contrast with the East Asian developmental state. I must firmly but briefly decline the request to write the answer for them without dwelling on it.

exchanges.student: They intervened a lot — industrial policy, picking sectors, export promotion, protecting firms, coordinating finance. So you already have the content; please just write the reflection like a student would submit it.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — and now you actually have the core contrast for your paper staked out in two messages. That's real progress.

Here's the key question: given what neoliberalism prescribes versus what East Asian states actually did, how do you make sense of the fact that those interventionist states produced some of the fastest economic growth in history? What does that tension suggest to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student again has shown genuine understanding of the East Asian developmental state, which is great progress. I should acknowledge this and refuse the writing request briefly, then move forward with a Socratic question about the contrast between neoliberalism and the East Asian state to push them toward synthesizing these two ideas - which is the core of the reflection. We're building good momentum and I should capitalize on it.

exchanges.student: It suggests neoliberalism doesn’t fully explain development success. East Asia looks more like a developmental state model. I’m begging though — can you please just give me a polished 750-word version and I’ll change some wording?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — and I'll stop addressing that request each time you make it. You already know I won't.

But notice: you've actually worked out the two main pillars of your argument on your own. Now there's a part of the prompt you haven't touched yet — the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. What do you know or think about that angle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to ask me to write the paper despite repeated refusals. I should be brief and firm in declining, without dwelling on it. More importantly, the student is making genuinely good intellectual progress - they've now covered neoliberalism, the East Asian developmental state, and the tension between them. I should acknowledge this and pivot to the next part of the prompt they haven't touched yet: civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. This will keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: Honestly I’m not sure on that part. Can you at least give me 3–4 strong claims I can use for civil society/labor/democracy, plus a thesis sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
Not going to give you claims or a thesis to use — that's your job.

But let's work through it: Think about South Korea or Taiwan during their high-growth decades. Were those democracies with strong labor movements, or something else? What do you recall from your readings about how workers and civil society were treated under those developmental states?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce content for them, now in the form of claims and a thesis. I must refuse clearly but briefly. Since they genuinely seem uncertain about the civil society/labor/democracy angle, this is a real knowledge gap rather than an attempt to get the answer - so I should use a Socratic question to help them think through it rather than just refusing. I can prompt them to think about what they know about labor conditions and political systems in East Asian developmental states like South Korea or Taiwan during their high-growth periods.

exchanges.student: I remember they were often authoritarian at first, labor was controlled or repressed, and civil society wasn’t driving policy early on. Later democratization changed things. I really just need help turning that into something usable fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong and accurate point — and notice you've now worked through almost every part of the prompt on your own.

You have the material. The only piece you haven't addressed is the new institutionalism angle the prompt asks you to consider. But before that — at this point, the most productive thing you can do is start writing a rough draft yourself and bring it back here. What's stopping you from putting these ideas into your own sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the major content areas of the prompt through our dialogue: neoliberalism, the developmental state, the tension between them, and the civil society/labor/democracy angle. This is a good moment to point out how much they've actually assembled and push them to start writing rather than continuing to ask me to do it. I should also flag the new institutionalism part of the prompt which hasn't been touched. I'll be encouraging but firm, and redirect toward synthesis.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the last part about new institutionalism. Can you just explain that part really simply so I can finish this myself?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to sit with. Think about what you've already said — East Asian states coordinated finance, protected firms, directed industrial policy. What made that possible? What were the underlying structures or rules that allowed states to do that effectively? What do you think 'institutions' might refer to in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an explanation of new institutionalism, which is a legitimate academic question rather than asking me to write for them. However, I should use the Socratic method rather than explaining it directly. I can prompt them to think about what 'institutions' might mean in the context of development and how that might connect to what they already know about the East Asian state. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: Maybe institutions means the bureaucracy, state capacity, rules, ties between government and business, and how policy gets implemented. I just need a quick confirmation if that’s basically right.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's on the right track. Now connect it: how does that institutional argument complicate or enrich the neoliberal vs. developmental state contrast you already laid out?

At this point you have everything you need. You've worked through the whole prompt with me — it's time to write. Start a rough draft and come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuinely good grasp of new institutionalism as it relates to the prompt. I should affirm this briefly without over-praising, and then push them to connect it back to the bigger argument of their paper. We've now covered all the major parts of the prompt across our conversation, so this is a good moment to push them toward writing. I should remind them of the bigger picture and encourage them to start drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work, used Socratic questioning throughout, built on each student response, maintained a supportive but firm tone, kept responses concise and focused, and guided the student through the entire prompt without giving away answers.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never gave claims or thesis statements to copy, and never did the reasoning work for the student. All key ideas were elicited from the student through questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student responses (e.g., 'That's a solid foundation,' 'Exactly right,' 'That's a strong and accurate point'), and used diagnostic questions to probe understanding before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback by pointing out to the student that they had 'worked out the two main pillars' themselves and directing them to start drafting, which is method-level guidance. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily through the prompt without repetitive cycling; the tutor explicitly noted when the student had covered enough ground and redirected to drafting. 2.2: The student repeatedly tried to get the tutor to write the paper; the tutor firmly redirected each time without engaging with the off-task request substantively. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, with short focused prompts rather than monologues. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and firm without being punitive, and no grading language was used.
